attempts.student_answer:
Okay, this is a craft-analysis question — how the author's choices shape the audience — and the course did teach me analytical claims about craft (technique + effect on reader), so I'll try. One honest problem up front: the question says "in this excerpt," but I don't actually have the excerpt printed in front of me. I know some famous lines from this speech, so I'll use those, but I'm flagging that I'm working from memory of famous quotes, not from a provided text.

Here's my attempt:

Although the delegates at the Virginia Convention could reasonably hope that more debate or petitions might still avoid war, Patrick Henry uses rhetorical questions, repetition, and direct appeals to convince them that waiting is no longer a real option, because each device pushes the listeners to feel that the decision has already been made for them by Britain's actions.

First, the rhetorical questions. Henry stacks up questions the audience cannot answer without agreeing with him — questions like whether they should keep hoping when their petitions have already been "slighted" and whether the British fleets and armies are there for any purpose besides forcing them into submission. A rhetorical question is a craft choice, not just content: instead of telling the delegates "Britain means war," he makes THEM supply that answer in their own heads. That matters because a conclusion the audience reaches themselves feels like their own conviction, not Henry's. It moves them from listening to a debate toward already having decided.

Second, repetition. The most famous example is the repeated "we must fight! I repeat it, sir, we must fight!" — he literally says "I repeat it," calling attention to the repetition. He also repeats the pattern of "we have petitioned... we have remonstrated... we have supplicated" (listing everything the colonists already tried). The effect of that repeated "we have" structure is to close off the escape route: every peaceful option gets named and shown as already used up and already failed. So when he lands on "we must fight," it sounds like the only step left, not one option among many. The repetition of "fight" itself hammers the decision so it can't be softened or postponed.

Third, the appeals to his listeners. Henry appeals to their honor and their fear at the same time. He asks whether life is "so dear, or peace so sweet, as to be purchased at the price of chains and slavery" — that's an appeal to their pride and values, framing further delay not as caution but as choosing slavery. And the ending, "give me liberty, or give me death," is an emotional appeal where he stakes his own life on the choice, which pressures the listeners: if he's willing to die for this, continuing to debate looks cowardly by comparison. He also addresses them respectfully ("sir," acknowledging the patriotism of the men who disagree with him), which is a real concession — he concedes his opponents are honorable — so the audience doesn't feel attacked and stays open to being persuaded.

Putting it together: the three devices work as a chain. The concession and respectful address keep the hesitant delegates listening; the rhetorical questions make them privately admit that peace has failed; the repetition eliminates every alternative until "fight" is the only word left standing; and the final appeal makes the choice a matter of personal honor. Henry isn't just informing the Convention — he's engineering the moment where debate ends, so that voting to prepare for war feels like the audience's own inevitable conclusion.

The upshot (trying to land it, not just repeat my claim): Henry's craft turns a policy debate into a moral ultimatum — by the end of the speech, the delegates aren't deciding WHETHER to fight but whether they are the kind of men who would refuse to.

Honesty note: my quotes are from memory of the famous lines of this speech, since the excerpt itself wasn't included with the question. If the actual excerpt was a different section, my specific evidence might not match it, even though my method (technique → effect on the audience → why it moves them toward the decision) is what the course taught me.

attempts.student_answer:
Okay, so I actually know how to do this KIND of essay. The course drilled me on exactly this: write a counterclaim-aware claim ('Although X, Y because Z'), concede the other side's strongest objection, answer it with a fact from a source plus a reason (not just a reason alone), pull evidence from multiple sources, and end with why it matters — the significance/upshot move.

My plan would be: (1) POSITION — 'Although raising the federal minimum wage to $15 could strain some small businesses, it should be raised because [reason that answers that objection].' (2) Evidence from Source 1 and Source 2, woven together, showing where they agree or push against each other. (3) CONCEDE the critics' strongest objection — probably job losses or small-business costs — and ANSWER it with a specific fact from the sources plus a reason that stays on the objection's own terms. (4) A conclusion that lands the upshot for workers and businesses instead of just repeating my claim.

But here's my problem, and I'm not going to fake it: the question says 'weighing both sources' and 'specific evidence from both sources,' and there are no sources here. I don't have any texts about the minimum wage in front of me. The course was really strict that answering an objection needs a FACT from the source plus a reason — a name-and-drop or a made-up statistic doesn't count. If I invent numbers like 'a study says X million workers would get a raise,' I'd be doing exactly the fake-evidence thing the lessons trained me to catch. So I can write the skeleton and the moves, but I can't write the actual essay with 'specific evidence from both sources' when the sources weren't provided to me. If someone hands me the two source texts, I'm confident I could do this — it's basically lessons 15 through 24 on a new topic.
attempts.missing_skill: The sources themselves were never given to me — the question says 'weighing both sources' and 'specific evidence from both sources,' but no source texts are attached to this test question, so I have nothing to pull facts from.

attempts.student_answer:
Okay, so this is the kind of thing I've been practicing since around lesson 15 — take a position across two sources, concede the strongest objection, then answer it with a fact plus a reason. One problem: the test says "weighing both sources" and "evidence from both sources," but I can't actually see the two sources anywhere in the question. So I'm going to write the essay the way the course taught me, and I'll mark where the source evidence goes — I'll describe the KIND of evidence each source would need to give, since I genuinely don't have the texts in front of me. I'm not going to fake quotes; lesson 16 drilled into me that evidence has to come from the actual source pool.

Here's my essay:

---

Although a national minimum age of 16 for social media would take real social connection away from younger teens who rely on it — and that loss is genuine, especially for isolated kids — the United States should still set the minimum at 16, because the documented harms to developing adolescents outweigh benefits that can be met in safer ways.

Both sources point to the same underlying problem even when they disagree about the solution. [Source 1 — the pro-restriction source — provides the evidence about mental health harms: rising rates of anxiety and depression among heavy adolescent users, and the point that platforms are designed to maximize engagement in ways young brains are especially vulnerable to.] That is not just content; it's a fact pattern that supports my position directly. [Source 2 — the anti-restriction source — provides the evidence I have to weigh against my side: that social media gives teens community, especially teens who feel alone in their schools, and that age limits are hard to enforce.] Weighing these, the harm evidence is about the whole population of young teens, while the benefit evidence is about a subset — and even that subset's need for connection doesn't require an algorithm-driven feed to be met.

The strongest objection my opposition could raise is not enforcement (that's the easy one — critics of my side always lead with "kids will lie about their age," but a rule being imperfectly enforced doesn't make it worthless; we don't repeal driving ages because some kids drive early). The strongest objection is the one Source 2 makes about isolated teens: for a kid in a small town who is the only one like them, online community can be a lifeline, and a ban until 16 cuts that lifeline during exactly the years they need it most. That objection deserves a fair concession, and I concede it: those teens exist, their need is real, and Source 2's evidence about connection is not fake.

But here is my answer, on the objection's own terms — staying with those isolated teens, not changing the subject. What fixes the conceded problem without giving up my side? A minimum age of 16 restricts algorithm-driven social media ACCOUNTS, not the internet. Isolated teens can still use moderated forums, group messaging, and interest communities that don't run on engagement-maximizing feeds — the specific design feature [Source 1] identifies as the harm mechanism. In other words, the objection is about connection, and connection survives the rule; what the rule removes is the feed engineered to keep a 13-year-old scrolling. If the lifeline argument were really about connection, it would be answered by these alternatives. If it's about access to the feed itself, then it's conceding [Source 1's] point about what the feed does.

The upshot — and I'm still not sure I know how to "land the upshot" since the course named it but never taught it, so here's my best try — is this: the choice isn't between connected teens and protected teens. It's between connection through a machine built to exploit them and connection without it. Setting the minimum at 16 chooses the second, and that's why my position holds even after giving the other side its best point.

---

If they had been, the bracketed spots are exactly where I'd put the named facts, the way lessons 15–19 made me do with the congestion pricing sources.

Every single lesson was either argument (concede-and-hold, answer the objection with a fact) or analysis of an author's craft (how a device creates an effect). I know how to pull evidence from a source — lessons 15-20 drilled that — so I could probably grab quotes about the parks. But I don't know how you're supposed to organize an essay when there's no claim to defend and no craft to analyze. Do I just make each question (what/why/challenges) its own paragraph? Is my 'thesis' just... a sentence saying what I'm about to explain? That feels like the 'summary not analysis' trap the course kept warning me about, except here summary might actually be the point? I genuinely don't know. Also I don't have the actual article text in front of me anyway, so I can't pull the 'specific evidence' the prompt demands. If I had to fake it I'd write three paragraphs answering the three questions with facts from the article, but I'd be guessing at the structure the whole time — the course never showed me an informational composition, a worked example of one, or a checklist for one. I'm not going to fake a full essay when I don't have the article or the skill.
attempts.missing_skill: Informational/explanatory composition writing — the whole course taught me argument and analysis moves (concede-and-hold claims, answering objections with source facts, craft claims about an author's choices), but never how to write a piece that just EXPLAINS a topic: no lesson covered organizing an informational essay around what/why/challenges categories, summarizing and paraphrasing an article's information neutrally, or structuring body paragraphs by topic instead of by claim-evidence-counterargument
